results.title_inferred: Ráo Huā (Wikstroemia flower) — Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰蕘者，饒也。其花繁饒也。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰蕘花生咸陽川谷及河南中牟。六月採花，陰乾。

弘景曰︰中牟者，時從河上來，形似芫花而極細，白色。

恭曰︰苗似胡荽，莖無刺。花細，黃色，四月、五月收，與芫花全不相似也。

保升曰︰所在有之，以雍州者為好。生岡原上，苗高二尺許。

宗奭曰︰今京洛間甚多。

時珍曰︰按蘇頌《圖經》言︰絳州所出芫花黃色，謂之黃芫花。其圖小株，花成簇生，恐即此蕘花也。生時色黃，乾則如白，故陶氏言細白也。或言無蕘花，以桃花代之，取其利耳。

【氣味】苦，寒，有毒。《別錄》曰︰辛，微寒，有毒。

【主治】傷寒溫瘧，下十二水，破積聚大堅症瘕，蕩滌腸胃中留癖，飲食、寒熱邪氣，利水道（《本經》）。療痰飲咳嗽（《別錄》）。治咳逆上氣，喉中腫滿，疰氣蠱毒，癖氣塊。

【發明】宗奭曰︰張仲景《傷寒論》以蕘花治利者，取其行水也。水去則利止，其意如此。今用之當斟酌，不可過使與不及也。須有是証乃用之。

好古曰︰仲景小青龍湯云︰若微利，去麻黃，加蕘花如雞子大，熬令赤色。用之蓋利水也。

時珍曰︰蕘花，蓋亦芫花之類，氣味主治大略相近。
results.translation:
**[Explanation of Name]** Li Shizhen states: The word 'ráo' [蕘] means 'abundant' [饒]. This refers to the profuse and abundant blooming of its flowers.

**[Collected Commentaries]** The *Bielu* [*Supplementary Records*] states: Ráo huā grows in the river valleys of Xianyang and in Zhongmu, Henan. The flowers are gathered in the sixth month and dried in the shade.

Tao Hongjing [Hongjing] states: The variety from Zhongmu occasionally comes by way of the river [to market]. In form it resembles yuán huā [Daphne genkwa], but is much more delicate, and white in color.

[Su] Gong states: The young plant resembles coriander [húsuī]; the stem has no thorns. The flowers are small and yellow; they are collected in the fourth and fifth months. It is not at all similar to yuán huā.

[Han] Baosheng states: It is found in many places, though the variety from Yongzhou is considered the best. It grows on hillside plains, and the plant reaches about two feet in height.

[Kou] Zongshi states: It is now very common in the region of the capital [Dongjing/Luoyang].

Li Shizhen states: According to Su Song's *Illustrated Canon* [*Tujing Bencao*], the yuán huā produced in Jiangzhou is yellow in color and is called 'yellow yuán huā' [huáng yuán huā]. The illustration shows a small plant with flowers growing in clusters—this is likely the same as the present ráo huā. The flowers are yellow when fresh but appear whitish when dried, which explains why Tao [Hongjing] described them as fine and white. Some sources say that when ráo huā is unavailable, peach blossom [táo huā] may be substituted, since both share the purgative property.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Bitter; cold; toxic. The *Bielu* states: Acrid; slightly cold; toxic.

**[Principal Indications]** Treats cold-damage fever [shānghán] and warm malaria [wēn nüè]; purges the twelve types of water-accumulation [十二水]; breaks up stubborn accumulations and masses [jī jù], hard concretions, and abdominal lumps [zhēng jiǎ]; flushes out retained aggregations [liú pǐ] in the stomach and intestines; eliminates pathogenic influences of food, cold, and heat; and promotes urination (*Shennong Bencao Jing*). Treats phlegm-fluid [tán yǐn] cough (*Bielu*). Treats cough with counterflow qi ascent, swelling and fullness in the throat, zhù-qi [注氣, malevolent contagious qi], gǔ toxin [蠱毒], and aggregation-qi lumps [pǐ qì kuài].

**[Elaboration]** Kou Zongshi states: Zhang Zhongjing's *Treatise on Cold Damage* [*Shānghán Lùn*] uses ráo huā to treat diarrhea by promoting the movement of water. When the water is expelled, the diarrhea ceases—such is the rationale. Today one should use it with careful judgment; it must neither be used in excess nor fall short of the proper amount. It should only be employed when the appropriate indication is present.

[Wang] Haogu states: Zhongjing's Minor Blue-Green Dragon Decoction [Xiǎo Qīnglóng Tāng] formula specifies: if there is mild diarrhea, remove má huáng and add ráo huā in a quantity about the size of a hen's egg, roasted until red. Its use here is likewise to promote the movement of water.

Li Shizhen states: Ráo huā is essentially a plant of the same type as yuán huā; their qi, flavor, and primary indications are broadly similar.

entities_mentioned.name: 蕘花 (ráo huā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wikstroemia canescens or a related Wikstroemia / Thymelaeaceae species; possibly also confused with Daphne species
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen suggests it may be closely related to or even the same as the yellow-flowered variety of yuán huā (Daphne genkwa). Some modern sources tentatively identify ráo huā as Wikstroemia canescens (Thymelaeaceae), but the taxonomy remains uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: 芫花 (yuán huā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Daphne genkwa Siebold & Zucc. (Thymelaeaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference plant throughout the entry.

entities_mentioned.name: 桃花 (táo huā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus persica (L.) Batsch — peach blossom
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a substitute for ráo huā on account of its shared purgative (laxative/diuretic) action.

entities_mentioned.name: 小青龍湯 (Xiǎo Qīnglóng Tāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Minor Blue-Green Dragon Decoction — classical TCM formula
entities_mentioned.notes: A compound decoction from Zhang Zhongjing's *Shānghán Lùn*. The entry records a modification in which ráo huā replaces má huáng when mild diarrhea is present.

results.translator_notes: 1. The 'twelve types of water' (十二水) is a classical Chinese medical category referring to twelve distinct patterns of pathological fluid accumulation, not a literal count of fluids. 2. The main indications section appears to be truncated in the source text (the entry for 癖氣塊 ends abruptly without a closing citation bracket); this has been preserved faithfully in both the cleaned original and the translation, with a note that the text is incomplete at that point. 3. The term 疰氣 (zhù qì) refers to a malevolent or contagious pathogenic influence traditionally associated with epidemic or supernatural illness. 4. OCR introduced a doubled bracket '《別錄》》' in the qi/flavor section; silently corrected to single brackets.

results.title_inferred: Zuiyucao (Butterfly Bush / Fish-Intoxicating Herb) — Flowers and Leaves
results.original_text:
【釋名】鬧魚花（《綱目》）、魚尾草（《綱目》）

【集解】時珍曰：醉魚草南方處處有之。多在塹岸邊，作小株生，高者三、四尺。根狀如八月開花成穗，紅紫色，儼如芫花一樣。結細子。漁人采花及葉以毒魚，盡圉圉而死，呼為醉魚兒草。池沼邊不可種之。此花色狀、氣味並如芫花，毒魚亦同。但花開不同時為異爾。按《中山經》云：熊耳山有草焉，其狀如蘇而赤華，名曰葶苧，可以毒魚。其此草之類歟？

花、葉

【氣味】辛、苦，溫，有小毒。

【主治】痰飲成齁，遇寒便發，取花研末，和米粉作果，炙熟食之，即效。又治誤食石斑魚子中毒，吐不止，及諸魚骨鯁者，搗汁，和冷水少許咽之，吐即止，骨即化也。久瘧成癖者，以花填鯽魚腹中，濕紙裹煨熟，空心食之，仍以花和海粉搗貼，便消（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Names:** Nao-yu-hua [Fish-Rousing Flower] (*Bencao Gangmu*); Yu-wei-cao [Fish-Tail Herb] (*Bencao Gangmu*).

**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: Zuiyucao [Fish-Intoxicating Herb] is found everywhere in the southern regions. It grows mostly along the banks of moats and ditches, forming small shrubs, the tallest reaching three to four feet in height. The root resembles [that of similar shrubs; text appears partially garbled here — see translator's notes]. It flowers in the eighth month, producing spikes of reddish-purple blooms that closely resemble those of Yuanhua [*Daphne genkwa*]. It bears small seeds. Fishermen collect the flowers and leaves to poison fish; the fish become stupefied and die, and so the plant is called Zuiyu-er-cao [Little Fish-Intoxicating Herb]. It should not be planted near ponds or marshes. The color, appearance, and smell of this plant's flowers are all like those of Yuanhua, and it similarly poisons fish. The only difference is that the two do not flower at the same time. According to the *Classic of the Mountains and Seas (Zhongshan Jing)*: 'On Mount Xiong'er there grows a plant resembling perilla [*Perilla frutescens*] but with red flowers, called Tingzhu, which can be used to poison fish.' Might this be of the same kind as the present herb?

**Flowers and Leaves**

**Qi and Flavor:** Acrid [xin], bitter [ku], warm [wen], mildly toxic [you xiao du].

**Indications and Treatments:**

1. **Phlegm-fluid accumulation causing wheezing [tan yin cheng hou]**, which flares upon exposure to cold: Take the flowers, grind them into a powder, mix with rice flour to form a cake, roast until cooked, and eat. This produces immediate relief.

2. **Accidental poisoning from consuming the roe of the grouper [shibanyu]**, causing incessant vomiting; and also for **fish bones lodged in the throat**: Pound the herb to extract the juice, mix with a small amount of cold water, and swallow. The vomiting will stop and the bone will dissolve.

3. **Chronic malaria forming a malarial mass [jiue cheng pi]**: Fill the belly of a crucian carp with the flowers, wrap in wet paper, and roast until cooked; eat on an empty stomach. Additionally, pound the flowers together with haifei [processed cuttlefish/sea powder — *hai fen*] and apply as a poultice. The mass will then disperse.

*(Li Shizhen)*

entities_mentioned.name: 醉魚草 (Zuiyucao; also 鬧魚花, 魚尾草)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Buddleja lindleyana or Buddleja davidii (Butterfly Bush, family Scrophulariaceae/Buddlejaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of reddish-purple spike-like flowers, fish-intoxicating properties, and growth habit along riverbanks strongly matches Buddleja lindleyana or a closely related Buddleja species native to southern China. The plant contains aucubin and other iridoid glycosides toxic to fish.

entities_mentioned.name: 芫花 (Yuanhua)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Daphne genkwa (Lilac Daphne, family Thymelaeaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological and toxicological comparison to Zuiyucao.

entities_mentioned.name: 葶苧 (Tingzhu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited from the Zhongshan Jing (Classic of the Mountains and Seas) as a fish-poisoning herb resembling perilla with red flowers from Mount Xiong'er. Exact identification uncertain; Li Shizhen tentatively groups it with Zuiyucao.

entities_mentioned.name: 石斑魚 (Shibanyu, grouper)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Epinephelus spp. (Grouper, family Serranidae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced for accidental roe poisoning, treated with Zuiyucao juice.

entities_mentioned.name: 鯽魚 (Jiyu, crucian carp)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carassius auratus (Crucian Carp)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle to administer the flowers in the treatment of chronic malarial masses.

entities_mentioned.name: 海粉 (Haifei / Hai fen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Processed cuttlefish bone powder (Sepia spp.) or marine mineral powder
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a topical poultice ingredient combined with Zuiyucao flowers. Exact substance uncertain; may refer to processed cuttlefish bone (Wu zei gu) or a type of sea mineral.

results.translator_notes:
1. The sentence beginning '根狀如' (the root resembles…) is incomplete or garbled in the source text, likely an OCR lacuna; the original presumably compared the root to that of a known plant. This has been flagged but not fabricated in the translation.
2. '痰飲成齁' (tan yin cheng hou): The character '齁' (hou) refers to a wheezing or rattling sound in the throat caused by phlegm accumulation — rendered here as 'phlegm-fluid accumulation causing wheezing.' The source text shows '齁' corrupted as an unusual character (圉圉 appears in an unrelated context referring to fish dying in a stupor, which is correctly placed).
3. '圉圉而死' literally means the fish 'struggled and died' (圉圉 describing the floundering of poisoned fish), which has been rendered as 'become stupefied and die.'
4. The Zhongshan Jing (中山經) citation is from the *Classic of the Mountains and Seas* (Shanhai Jing), specifically the chapter on the Central Mountains.

results.original_text:
【釋名】草（音罔）、芒草（《山海經》）、鼠莽。

弘景曰︰莽本作 字，俗訛呼爾。

時珍曰︰此物有毒，食之令人迷罔，故名。山人以毒鼠，謂之鼠莽。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰莽草生上谷山谷及冤句。五月採葉，陰乾。

弘景曰︰今東間處處皆有，葉青辛烈者良。人用搗以和陳粟米粉，納水中，魚吞即死浮
出，人取食之無妨。

頌曰︰今南中州郡及蜀川皆有之。木若石南而葉稀，無花實。五月、七月採葉，陰乾。
一說︰藤生，繞木石間。既謂之草，乃蔓生者是也。

宗奭曰︰莽草諸家皆謂之草，而本草居木部。今世所用，皆木葉如石南葉，枝梗乾則皺，揉之其臭如椒。

曰︰凡用葉，勿用尖及攣生者。

  "title_inferred": "Mǎng Cǎo (莽草) — Illicium lanceolatum, a toxic plant used in external treatments",
  "original_text": "【釋名】莽草（音罔）、芒草（《山海經》）、鼠莽。

弘景曰：莽本作罔字，俗訛呼爾。

時珍曰：此物有毒，食之令人迷罔，故名。山人以毒鼠，謂之鼠莽。

【集解】《別錄》曰：莽草生上谷山谷及冤句。五月採葉，陰乾。

弘景曰：今東間處處皆有，葉青辛烈者良。人用搗以和陳粟米粉，納水中，魚吞即死浮出，人取食之無妨。

頌曰：今南中州郡及蜀川皆有之。木若石南而葉稀，無花實。五月、七月採葉，陰乾。一說：藤生，繞木石間。既謂之草，乃蔓生者是也。

宗奭曰：莽草諸家皆謂之草，而本草居木部。今世所用，皆木葉如石南葉，枝梗乾則皺，揉之其臭如椒。

曰：凡用葉，勿用尖及攣生者。

時珍曰：《范子計然》云：莽草出三輔，青色者善。

葉

【氣味】辛，溫，有毒。普曰：神農：辛；雷公、桐君：苦，有毒。

時珍曰：莽草製雌黃、雄黃而有毒，誤食害人。惟紫河車磨水服，及黑豆煮汁服，可解。豆汁澆其根即爛，性相製也。

【主治】風頭癰腫，乳癰疝瘕，除結氣疥瘙。殺蟲魚（《本經》）。療喉痺不通，乳難。頭風癢，可用沐，勿令入眼（《別錄》）。治風疽，疝氣腫墜凝血，治瘰癧，除濕風，不入湯服。主頭瘡白禿殺蟲。與白蘞、赤小豆為末，雞子白調如糊，敷毒腫，乾更易上（甄權）。治皮膚麻痺，煎濃湯淋。風蟲牙痛（大明）。

【發明】頌曰：古方治風毒痺厥諸酒，皆用莽草。今醫家取葉煎湯，熱含少頃吐之，治牙齒風蟲及喉痺甚效。

宗奭曰：濃煎湯，淋渫皮膚麻痺。《周禮》翦氏掌除蠹物，以莽草熏之則死。

results.title_inferred: Yinyu (茵芋): Identification, Collection, Properties, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】莞草（《別錄》）、卑共（《別錄》）。

時珍曰︰茵芋本作因預，未詳其義。莞草與莆莞名同。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰茵芋生太山川谷。三月三日采葉，陰乾。

弘景曰︰好者出彭城，今近道亦有。莖葉狀似莽草而細軟，連細莖采之。方用甚稀，惟合療風酒。

大明曰︰出自海鹽。形似石南，樹生，葉濃，五、六、七月採。

頌曰︰今雍州、絳州、華州、杭州亦有之。春生苗，高三、四尺，莖赤。葉似石榴而短濃，又似石南葉。四月開細白花，五月結實。三月、四月、七月採莖葉，日乾。

莖、葉

【氣味】苦，溫，有毒。《別錄》曰︰微溫，有毒。

權曰︰苦、辛，有小毒。

【主治】五臟邪氣，心腹寒熱，羸瘦，如瘧狀，發作有時，諸關節風濕痺痛（《本經》）。療久風濕，走四肢，腳弱（《別錄》）。治男子女人軟腳毒風，拘急攣痛（甄權）。一切冷風，筋骨怯弱羸顫。入藥炙用。

【發明】時珍曰︰《千金》、《外台》諸古方，治風癇有茵芋丸；治風痺有茵芋酒；治婦人產後中風有茵芋膏，風濕諸方多用之。茵芋、石南、莽草皆古人治風妙品，而近世罕知，亦醫家疏缺也。
results.translation:
**Nomenclature**
Alternative names: Wǎncǎo [莞草] (Bielu), Bèigòng [卑共] (Bielu).

Li Shizhen states: The name 茵芋 (yīnyù) was originally written 因預 (yīnyù); the meaning of this form is not fully understood. The name Wǎncǎo is shared with the plant known as Púwǎn [莆莞].

**Collected Commentaries**
The Bielu states: Yinyu grows in the river valleys of Mt. Tai [Taishan]. Leaves are collected on the third day of the third month and dried in the shade.

Tao Hongjing states: The finest specimens come from Pengcheng; it is also found in nearby localities today. The stems and leaves resemble those of Mangcao [莽草, Illicium lanceolatum] but are more slender and soft; it is collected together with its fine stems. It is rarely used in prescriptions, being employed chiefly in medicinal wind-treatment wines [療風酒].

Da Ming states: It comes from Haiyan. In form it resembles Shinan [石南, Photinia/Pieris], grows as a shrub, with thick leaves; collected in the fifth, sixth, and seventh months.

Su Song states: It is also found today in Yongzhou, Jiangzhou, Huazhou, and Hangzhou. Shoots emerge in spring; the plant grows three to four chi tall with red stems. The leaves resemble those of the pomegranate but are shorter and thicker, and also resemble the leaves of Shinan. Fine white flowers open in the fourth month; fruit sets in the fifth month. Stems and leaves are collected in the third, fourth, and seventh months and sun-dried.

**Stems and Leaves**

**Qi and Flavor**
Bitter, warm, toxic. The Bielu states: slightly warm, toxic. Zhen Quan states: bitter and acrid, slightly toxic.

**Principal Indications**
— Pathogenic [xié] qi of the five viscera, cold and heat in the heart and abdomen, emaciation, malarial-like episodes occurring at fixed intervals, and wind-damp [fēngshī] bi-obstruction pain in all the joints (Bencao Jing [Shennong's Classic]).
— Treatment of chronic wind-dampness spreading through the four limbs and causing weakness of the legs (Bielu).
— Treatment in both men and women of soft-leg toxic wind [軟腳毒風], with contracture, cramping, and pain (Zhen Quan).
— All cold-wind [lěngfēng] conditions, weakness and trembling of the sinews and bones. Processed by roasting [炙] before use in medicinal preparations.

**Elucidation**
Li Shizhen states: Among the ancient prescriptions of the Qianjin [Fang] and the Waitai [Miyao], Yinyu Pills [茵芋丸] are used to treat wind epilepsy [fēngxián]; Yinyu Wine [茵芋酒] is used to treat wind bi-obstruction [fēngbì]; Yinyu Paste [茵芋膏] is used to treat wind stroke [zhòngfēng] occurring in women after childbirth; and yinyu appears in numerous formulas for wind-dampness conditions. Yinyu, Shinan, and Mangcao were all prized remedies for wind disorders among the ancients, yet they are rarely known in recent times — a lacuna in the knowledge of physicians.

entities_mentioned.name: 茵芋 (Yīnyù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Skimmia reevesiana (Rutaceae) or closely related Skimmia japonica; historically identified with Skimmia reevesiana Fortune
entities_mentioned.notes: The plant description — shrub with red stems, thick leaves resembling pomegranate or Photinia, small white flowers — is broadly consistent with Skimmia reevesiana. Some scholars also propose Illicium-related taxa, but the morphological details better fit Skimmia. The plant is known to contain toxic alkaloids consistent with the 'toxic' classification.

entities_mentioned.name: 莽草 (Mǎngcǎo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Illicium lanceolatum A.C.Sm. (Schisandraceae/Illiciaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a morphological comparator and co-listed wind remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: 石南 (Shínán)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Photinia serrulata Lindl. or Pieris japonica (Ericaceae); traditionally Photinia davidsoniae
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as morphological comparator for leaf shape and growth habit; also cited as a classical wind remedy alongside yinyu.

entities_mentioned.name: 茵芋丸 (Yīnyù Wán)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical pill formulation for wind epilepsy, referenced from Qianjin Fang and Waitai Miyao.

entities_mentioned.name: 茵芋酒 (Yīnyù Jiǔ)
entities_mentioned.notes: A medicinal wine preparation for wind bi-obstruction [風痺]; also referenced in the Collected Commentaries as the primary classical use of the plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 茵芋膏 (Yīnyù Gāo)
entities_mentioned.notes: A paste preparation for post-partum wind stroke in women.

results.translator_notes: 1. The original text's '入藥炙用' (roast before medicinal use) concludes the indications section somewhat abruptly, likely a marginal annotation incorporated into the text; it is translated in place as consistent with the passage structure. 2. '軟腳毒風' (soft-leg toxic wind) is a classical clinical term denoting a condition resembling modern beriberi or peripheral neuropathy with weakness and contracture; rendered with the original term bracketed. 3. The geographic place names (Yongzhou, Jiangzhou, Huazhou, Hangzhou, Pengcheng) have been left in pinyin with no modern equivalents supplied, as their historical boundaries differ from modern administrative divisions. 4. Li Shizhen's note that the original graph was 因預 rather than 茵芋 is a bibliographic observation about variant character forms, not a semantic etymology; translated accordingly.

【釋名】地椹（《本經》）、天豆（《別錄》）、石能（《別錄》）、魯果能（《本經》）、水堇（《吳普》。音謹，又音芹）、苦堇（《爾雅》）、堇葵（郭璞）、胡椒菜（《救荒》）、彭根（《別錄》）。

弘景曰︰生於石上，其葉芮芮短小，故名。

恭曰︰實如桑椹，故名地椹。

禹錫曰︰《爾雅》云︰嚙，苦堇也。郭璞云︰即堇葵也。本草言味甘，而此云苦者，古人語倒，猶甘草謂之大苦也。

時珍曰︰芮芮，細貌。其椹之子細芮，故名。地椹以下，皆子名也。水堇以下，皆苗名也。苗作蔬食，味辛而滑，故有椒、葵之名。《唐本草》菜部堇系重出，今依《吳普本草》
合並為一。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰石龍芮，生太山川澤石邊。五月五日采子，二月、八月採皮，陰乾。

弘景曰︰今出近道。子形粗，似蛇床子而扁，非真好者，人言是蓄菜子也。東山石上所生者，其葉芮芮短小，其子狀如葶藶，黃色而味小辛，此乃是真也。

{
  "title_inferred": "Shi Long Rui (Buttercup / Ranunculus sceleratus): Names, Collection, Properties, and Uses",
  "original_text": "【釋名】地椹（《本經》）、天豆（《別錄》）、石能（《別錄》）、魯果能（《本經》）、水堇（《吳普》。音謹，又音芹）、苦堇（《爾雅》）、堇葵（郭璞）、胡椒菜（《救荒》）、彭根（《別錄》）。

弘景曰︰生於石上，其葉芮芮短小，故名。

恭曰︰實如桑椹，故名地椹。

禹錫曰︰《爾雅》云︰嚙，苦堇也。郭璞云︰即堇葵也。本草言味甘，而此云苦者，古人語倒，猶甘草謂之大苦也。

時珍曰︰芮芮，細貌。其椹之子細芮，故名。地椹以下，皆子名也。水堇以下，皆苗名也。苗作蔬食，味辛而滑，故有椒、葵之名。《唐本草》菜部堇系重出，今依《吳普本草》合並為一。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰石龍芮，生太山川澤石邊。五月五日采子，二月、八月採皮，陰乾。

弘景曰︰今出近道。子形粗，似蛇床子而扁，非真好者，人言是蓄菜子也。東山石上所生者，其葉芮芮短小，其子狀如葶藶，黃色而味小辛，此乃是真也。

恭曰︰今用者，俗名水堇。苗似附子，實如桑椹，生下濕地，五月熟，葉、子皆味辛。山南者粒大如葵。堇菜野生。藏器曰︰《爾雅》云︰芨，堇草。注云︰烏頭苗也。蘇恭注天雄亦云︰石龍芮葉似堇草，故名水堇。據此，則堇草是烏頭苗，水堇定是石龍芮，更非別物。頌曰︰今惟出兗州。一叢數莖，莖青紫色，每莖三葉，其葉短小多刻缺，子如葶藶而色黃。蘇恭所說乃水堇，非石龍芮也。兗州所生者，正與《本經》及陶氏說合。

宗奭曰︰石龍芮有兩種︰水中生者葉光而末圓，陸地生者葉毛而末銳。入藥須水生者。陸生者，又謂之天灸，而補陰不足，莖冷失精。

時珍曰︰蘇恭言水堇即石龍芮，蘇頌非之，非矣。按︰魏《吳普本草》石龍芮一名水堇，其說甚明。《唐本草》菜部所出水堇，言其苗也。《本經》石龍芮，言其子也。寇宗奭所言陸生者，乃是毛堇，有大毒，不可食。水堇，即俗稱胡椒菜者，處處一枝三葉。葉青而光滑，有三尖，多細缺。江淮人三、四月採苗，瀹過，晒蒸黑色為蔬。四、五月開細黃花，結小實，大如豆，狀如初生桑椹，青綠色。搓散則子甚細，如葶藶子，即石龍芮也。宜半老時采之。《范子計然》云︰石龍芮出三輔，色黃者善。

子（根皮同）

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。普曰︰神農︰苦，平；岐伯︰酸；扁鵲︰大寒；雷公︰咸，無毒。

之才曰︰大戟為之使，畏吳茱萸、蛇蛻皮。
甘，寒，無毒。時珍曰︰微辛、苦、澀。

【主治】風寒濕痺，心腹邪氣，利關節，止煩滿。久服輕身明目不老（《本經》）。平腎胃氣，補陰氣不足，失精莖冷。令人皮膚光澤有子（《別錄》）。逐諸風，除心熱躁（大明）。搗汁，洗馬毒瘡，並服之。又塗蛇蠍毒及癰腫（《唐本》）。久食除心下煩熱。主寒熱鼠瘻，瘰癧生瘡，結核聚氣，下瘀血，止霍亂。又生搗汁半升服。

【發明】詵曰︰堇葉止霍亂，與香薷同功。香薷即香薷也。

【發明】時珍曰︰石龍芮，乃平補之藥，古方多用之。其功與枸杞、覆盆子相埒，而世人不知用，何哉？",
  "translation": "**[Names and Nomenclature]**

Alternate names: Dì Shèn [Ground Mulberry] (Shennong Bencao Jing); Tiān Dòu [Heaven Bean] (Mingyi Bielu); Shí Néng (Bielu); Lǔ Guǒ Néng (Bencao Jing); Shuǐ Jǐn [Water Violet] (Wu Pu; pronounced 'jǐn,' also read as 'qín'); Kǔ Jǐn [Bitter Violet] (Erya); Jǐn Kuí [Violet Mallow] (Guo Pu); Hújiāo Cài [Pepper Vegetable] (Jiuhuang Bencao); Péng Gēn (Bielu).

Tao Hongjing says: It grows on rocks [shí], and its leaves are small and fine [ruì ruì], hence the name.

Su Gong says: Its fruit resembles mulberry drupes [sāng shèn], hence the name Dì Shèn [Ground Mulberry].

Yu Xi says: The Erya states: 'Niè is
